key_insight: Adjoint optimization is best for grid-based unknowns, while PINNs are most natural for neural-parameterized unknowns; the best choice depends primarily on how the inverse quantity is represented.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained inverse problems in mechanics and fluids, matching the solver to the parameterization can reduce cost and improve reconstruction quality, especially in time-dependent settings.
method: Built a fair apples-to-apples comparison using the same domains, PDEs, observations, regularization, optimizer, and precision where applicable.
Tested both approaches on Burgers, Darcy permeability inversion, Allen–Cahn reaction identification, and Navier–Stokes viscosity identification.
Also evaluated a hybrid PINN-warm-started adjoint strategy to combine faster initialization with adjoint-level accuracy.
evidence_signal: Across four benchmarks, the abstract reports that representation choice largely determines which method performs better, with adjoints favored for fields and PINNs competitive for neural representations and time-dependent cases.
watch_item: The comparison depends on careful matching of formulation details; in practice, storage/differentiation costs, optimizer settings, and parameterization choices can strongly affect outcomes.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
